era_context: The Syrian War (192-188 BCE) is in its third year and approaching its climax. Antiochus III has been pushed back to Asia Minor and is facing increasing pressure from Roman forces. Rome is preparing for a final decisive engagement. Egypt under Ptolemy V remains weak. China under Emperor Wen is in the later years of his reign (he will die in 157 BCE). The Hellenistic world is entering a period of Roman dominance.

events.title: Battle of Magnesia; decisive Roman victory over Antiochus III
events.region: Magnesia (Asia Minor, near modern Manisa, Turkey)
events.description: Roman forces under Lucius Cornelius Scipio Africanus (with his brother Publius Cornelius Scipio Nasica as legate) decisively defeat Antiochus III at the Battle of Magnesia. The battle is one of the largest and most significant battles of the Hellenistic period, with estimates of 70,000 troops on each side. Antiochus's forces are routed, and he is forced to flee the battlefield. The Roman victory is overwhelming and marks the end of Seleucid power in the Mediterranean. The battle establishes Rome as the undisputed dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and effectively ends the Syrian War. Antiochus will be forced to accept harsh peace terms in the Treaty of Apamea (188 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Magnesia in 190 BCE is one of the best-documented battles of the ancient world, with detailed accounts from multiple sources. The date is secure. Ancient sources provide extensive descriptions of the battle, including troop numbers, tactics, and casualties.

events.title: Antiochus III flees to Syria; end of active military operations
events.description: Following his defeat at Magnesia, Antiochus III flees to Syria, abandoning his forces in Asia Minor. The flight marks the effective end of active military operations in the Syrian War, though formal peace negotiations will continue for two more years. Antiochus's defeat is complete, and he is forced to accept that Seleucid power in the Mediterranean is at an end. The flight is a dramatic moment in the decline of Hellenistic power and the rise of Roman dominance.
events.certainty_note: The flight of Antiochus III from Asia Minor is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Roman consolidation of control over Asia Minor and the Aegean
events.region: Asia Minor and the Aegean (former Seleucid territories)
events.description: Following the Battle of Magnesia, Roman forces consolidate their control over Asia Minor and the Aegean. Roman commanders establish administrative structures and begin the process of integrating the conquered territories into the Roman sphere of influence. This consolidation marks the beginning of Rome's direct involvement in the governance of the eastern Mediterranean and sets the stage for the eventual creation of Roman provinces in Asia Minor. The consolidation is a significant moment in the transformation of the Mediterranean world from a Hellenistic to a Roman-dominated system.
events.certainty_note: The general process of Roman consolidation in 190 BCE is well-documented, but specific administrative details are not always clearly attested. The dating is secure within ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact troop numbers at the Battle of Magnesia are disputed. Ancient sources give figures ranging from 70,000 to 80,000 troops on each side, but modern scholars debate whether these numbers are accurate or inflated. Some sources suggest that Hannibal was not present at the battle, despite being an advisor to Antiochus III. The significance of the battle in ending Seleucid power is well-established, but some scholars argue that the Seleucid Empire remained a significant power in the eastern Mediterranean for several more decades. However, the overall narrative of the battle as a decisive Roman victory is not disputed.
historiographic_note: The Battle of Magnesia is one of the best-documented events of the ancient world, with detailed accounts from multiple sources, particularly Livy and Polybius. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Roman perspective, but Polybius had access to Seleucid sources and provides a relatively balanced account. The dating of the battle is secure. The battle is extensively studied by modern scholars and is a major focus of Hellenistic and Roman history.

graph_edges.note: The Roman victory at Magnesia forced Antiochus III to flee to Syria
The Roman victory at Magnesia enabled the consolidation of Roman control over Asia Minor
The Battle of Magnesia led directly to the Peace of Apamea, which formally ended the Syrian War
The Battle of Magnesia was the climactic engagement of the Syrian War that began in 192 BCE
